data.title: Diamonds In The Rough: 10 Emerging Markets Stocks To Buy Now
data.description:
Emerging Markets Rally in 2025

Grab your passport—it’s time to take a vacation from U.S. equities.

As the S&P 500 soars back to near-historic levels following heavy losses, investors are feeling optimistic that Washington’s 90-day trade truce with Beijing has helped mitigate much of the fallout from Donald Trump’s ‘Liberation Day’ tariffs. But the pain is far from over on Wall Street, as lingering trade tensions, inflation, and the threat of stagflation loom over the market. While volatile markets present plenty of opportunities to buy the dip on cheaply valued U.S. stocks with strong fundamentals, it’s also a good time to explore highly-rated stocks in economies less impacted by market uncertainty and the ongoing trade war.

A sweeping sell-off of U.S. equities, a weakening American dollar (DXY), increasing economic stimulus measures by China, and signs of bottoming earnings expectations have fueled a rare rally in emerging markets equities in 2025, with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (EEM) up more than 10% year-to-date. Bank of America Global Research analysts see a “surprisingly positive outlook” for emerging markets equities, as they continue to benefit from a weaker dollar and low trade war exposure. The Federal Reserve’s decision to hold interest rates until the tariff impact on inflation becomes clearer could also extend that window of opportunity for EM equities, BofA’s global economist Claudio Irigoyen noted at the end of last month.

Most Emerging Markets Remain Up YTD

A weakening dollar can boost emerging market equities mainly “through three mechanisms,” asset manager AllianceBernstein writes in a market outlook:

First, a weaker dollar can attract more inflows as investors seek higher returns in a depreciating-dollar environment, potentially stimulating corporate and economic growth as well. Moreover, bond issuers benefit when the cost to service their debt drops, since many EM sovereigns and corporate bonds are denominated in US dollars. Commodities prices, a key driver across many EM economies, also tend to rise when the dollar weakens—another potential tailwind.

Not everyone is confident that those tailwinds are here to stay, however. Despite the recent rally, Wells Fargo analysts remain skeptical on emerging markets equities. They noted political instability, corporate governance concerns, and signs of a strengthening dollar as a few reasons to proceed with caution. After all, the combined market value of the three largest publicly traded American companies—Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), and Nvidia (NVDA)—continues to outweigh that of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (EEM), which totaled around $7.9T as of April 30, 2025.

The U.S. Dollar Is Down 7.90% YTD, But Has Climbed 1.82% Over The Past Month

Investing in emerging markets is not an all-or-nothing approach, however. Selecting global stocks with excellent fundamentals and healthy balance sheets can help complement a diverse portfolio regardless of the dollar’s direction.

Investing in Emerging Markets with SA Quant

The market is full of emerging market stocks with solid growth rates at relatively cheap valuations. To scan for the best opportunities, I used SA’s Quant System to identify Strong Buy-rated companies operating outside the U.S. with robust balance sheets, strong growth rates, solid valuation frameworks, good profitability, increasing upward EPS Revisions by analysts, and excellent momentum.

Using a combination of powerful computer processing and what I like to call our special ‘Quantamental’ analysis, our proprietary algorithm separates the weak companies from the strong ones based on their historical financials, analysts' consensus growth estimates, and hundreds of financial metrics. Using these insights, it ranks them in terms of valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions, assigning letter grades to each category to help contextualize thousands of data points.

The result was 10 Strong Buy emerging markets stocks across a variety of industries, attractively priced with strong growth trajectories that were less impacted by recent market volatility. The below list was assembled using SA Quant’s portfolio construction tool.

SA’s Top 10 Emerging Markets Stocks, by Quant Rating

The top 10 non-U.S. stocks have median forward revenue growth of 8.5%, median forward EPS growth of 15.7%, and a median forward PEG ratio of 0.74x. These figures reflect foreign stocks’ tendency in the current climate to be cheaper and have higher growth potential than the broad U.S. market.

Let’s examine each stock in further detail.

1. Banco Santander-Chile (NYSE: BSAC)

  • Market Capitalization: $11.94B

  • Country: Chile

  • Sector: Financials

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 62 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 11 out of 66

Banco Santander-Chile (BSAC) is a Latin American lender that is often overlooked by international investors despite its strong fundamentals. A subsidiary of Spanish multinational Banco Santander, S.A. (SAN), BSAC has emerged as a key growth engine for Santander, defying an otherwise slowing Chilean economy and global trade uncertainty with a 131% year-over-year surge in net profit to CH$278B (US$290M), a 33.2% Y/Y increase in operating income and 25.6% return on equity.

The stock has returned 34% YTD, reaching a new 52-week high of $25.53 per share. BSAC boasts an attractive valuation. Its forward PEG of 0.71 is a 43% discount from the sector median, and its forward P/E of 10.96 is a 3% discount from the sector.

Forward revenue growth of 15.65% is a 154% difference to the sector median, and 96% ROE growth underlines a strong growth outlook. BSAC’s profitability is solid with a trailing net margin income of 44.8%, while momentum is above-average with six-month price performance of nearly 30% compared to the -4.25% sector median. My next selection offers a similarly powerful long-term growth outlook at a discounted price

2. Karooooo Ltd. (NASDAQ: KARO)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.65B

  • Country: Singapore

  • Sector: Technology

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 15 out of 542

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 189

Karooooo (KARO) is a SaaS mobility group with robust revenue growth, solid profitability, and fast-growing momentum thanks to its Cartrack and Logistics subscription-based suites of cloud-based services. The Singapore-based company’s most recent earnings beat expectations, with a 39% Y/Y increase in EPS to ZAR9.48 ($0.53). It also reported a 17% Y/Y increase in subscribers as it expands further into Southeast Asia, Africa, and Europe. As SA analyst Kennedy Njagi wrote at the end of last month:

KARO's AI-powered solutions enhance fleet management efficiency, safety, and operational excellence, positioning it well in the $334Bn global telematics market. Despite trading below peer multiples, KARO's solid financials, 47% EBITDA margins, and innovative product offerings suggest it is significantly undervalued.

KARO’s strides in AI technology in fleet management, including AI-powered cameras that observe driver behaviors and models that can predict equipment failures, have helped make it one of the most efficient services in its category. In addition to being undervalued compared to its direct competitors, KARO is also well-priced compared to the wider technology sector. Its forward P/E GAAP of 29 is a near 7% discount from the sector median. Momentum is strong despite global trade tensions and a sweeping tech sell-off in March and April, with a one-year price return of +80% vs. the S&P 500’s 10%

KARO vs. S&P 500 1-Yr Performance

My next selection is another Strong Buy from Asia that leverages the power of AI technology, this time in the fintech space.

3. Qifu Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ: QFIN)

  • Market Capitalization: $5.72B

  • Country: China

  • Sector: Financials

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 16 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 4 out of 37

Qifu Technology (QFIN) is an AI-powered credit platform operating under the Qifu Jietiao brand in China. Included among my Top Five Chinese Stocks To Ride Out The Trade War, the fintech group has made an impressive comeback after being down more than 15% after unveiling a “special action plan” to boost domestic spending. The increased economic stimulus, paired with continued improvements in QFIN’s AI capabilities and user acquisition has helped it achieve significant upside. As SA analyst Alpha Mantra recently wrote:

Qifu Technology shows strong performance with improving asset quality despite macroeconomic challenges, making it a promising long-term investment. QFIN’s mix of capital-heavy and capital-light business models, along with robust ROE, positions it well for growth in the Chinese credit tech market.

QFIN’s ‘A-’ valuation grade stands out amongst its financial peers. Its forward non-GAAP P/E of 5.95 represents a more than 44% discount to the sector median. While its ‘A+’ forward PEG of 0.41 comes at an impressive 68% discount to the sector.

The company’s increasing AI spend and growth tailwinds from Chinese economic stimulus are reflected by forward EPS GAAP growth of 30%, a 209% difference to the sector median. Profitability has remained at a solid ‘B+’ over the past five months, while A+ momentum is reflected by one-year price performance of +100%. With eight positive EPS and revenue revisions over the past 90 days and zero negative revisions in either category, analysts are similarly keen on this China bull.

My next pick is yet another Asian heavyweight touting unstoppable AI-powered growth.

4. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (NYSE: TSM)

  • Market Capitalization: $854.72B

  • Country: Taiwan

  • Sector: Technology

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 26 out of 542

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 68

I recently weighed the pros and cons of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) amid the surging Taiwanese dollar and Trump’s trade war, among other factors. The result? Those challenges pale compared to the Taiwanese chipmaker’s fantastic growth outlook and solid valuation. The world's largest chipmaker, which manufactures chips for tech giants including Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), has been on a roll following a 60% rise in net profit in Q1 2025. Cooling trade tensions between the U.S. and China and explosive demand for AI chips continue to fuel that momentum. As SA analyst Hunting Alpha writes on the "bizarrely undervalued” chipmaker:

TSMC is poised to gain market share from Samsung, with potential collaborations with US chip giants further strengthening its position…Reduced geopolitical risks and attractive valuations suggest potential for multiple expansion, making a compelling case for undervaluation of TSM stock.

Despite TSM’s D+ valuation, for a semiconductor company of its stature, the company offers a relative discount, as showcased by a forward PEG of 0.97, a -42% difference from the sector median, and its forward P/E GAAP of 21 is a 25% discount to the sector.

Highlights from its strong growth outlook include forward revenue growth of 28%, a +300% difference, and A+ dividend per share growth of 17%, a 236% difference. While the company offers a modest 1.35% trailing dividend yield, its track record of 20 consecutive years of dividend payments offers investors looking for income to offset inflation and tariffs a nice option.

Next up is a South American beverage company that is similarly undervalued compared to its powerful growth outlook.

5. Compañía Cervecerías Unidas S.A. (NYSE: CCU)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.58B

  • Country: Chile

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 12 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 7

Chilean beverage group Compañía Cervecerías Unidas (CCU) produces and distributes alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages throughout Latin America, with recognizable brands including Cristal beer in Chile and Córdoba beer in Argentina. The company has returned nearly 23% YTD despite a volatile global trade landscape, posting strong Q1 performance including a 10.7% Y/Y increase in net income and an 8.5% Y/Y increase in gross profit.

CCU Q1 2025 Earnings Results

That momentum has helped the company, while continuing to trade at a discount to many of its peers as the second-highest Quant-ranked consumer staples stock. CCU shares trade for less than $14. That low price comes with impressive value: the beverage maker has a forward PEG of 1.16, a 57% discount to the sector median among other attractive metrics.

Forward EPS diluted growth of 19%, a 142% difference from the sector median, underlines solid growth, while the three-month price performance of nearly 8% versus the sector median of 0.32% % reflects solid momentum. My next selection is another South American brewer offering quality beers and Quant Factor Grades.

6. Ambev S.A. (NYSE: ABEV)

  • Market Capitalization: $39.23B

  • Country: Brazil

  • Sector: Financials

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 2 out of 7

Ambev (ABEV) produces and distributes beers in South America under a variety of global brands including Budweiser, Presidente, Corona, and Modelo Especial. The company saw growth of its premium beer segment in Q1, driven higher by the celebratory Brazilian Carnival season. ABEV is also diversifying further into the non-alcoholic category with its Guaraná Antarctica Zero and Pepsi Black brands.

After facing pressure from investors to lower its expenditures, ABEV’s turnaround strategy has gained traction as the group has returned over 35% YTD. As SA analyst Valkyrie Trading Society wrote earlier this month:

CAPEX is lower by 18% YoY, and management is indicating that greater "discipline" in terms of CAPEX is going to be the new normal. Ambev continues to trade at a discount to peers, with an EV/EBITDA of around 6x compared to the sector average of around 10x.

ABEV boasts an excellent valuation with a forward GAAP P/E of 14.58, over a 24% discount from the sector median.

ABEV showcases solid growth metrics, including YoY Revenue growth of 15% vs the sector’s 1.7%. Profitability is a strong point, with a trailing net income margin of nearly 16% versus the sector mean 4%. ABEV also boasts a three-month price performance of 32%, an impressive 9,472% difference to the 0.32% sector median as many of its consumer staples peers have struggled in the current trade environment. I’ll cheers to that.

Up next is another Brazilian company with excellent fundamentals and performance that has been overlooked by the majority of the market.

7. TIM S.A. (NYSE: TIMB)

  • Market Capitalization: $8.52B

  • Country: Brazil

  • Sector: Communications

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 4 out of 229

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 1 out of 19

TIM (TIMB) is a Rio de Janeiro-based telecommunications company serving individuals and large corporations. The company posted strong performance in Q1, including a 4.9% Y/Y increase in net income to BRL 6.39B ($1.1B), continuing its extended rally as it has returned nearly 45% YTD. Priced at $17.51 per share while generating consistent profits, TIMB offers growth at a reasonable price to investors in search of geographical diversification and long-term value.

As SA analyst Bernard Zambonin summed up earlier this year: “TIM S.A. remains a strong investment with defensive appeal, solid dividends, and low volatility, despite economic challenges in Brazil and currency devaluation.”

TIMB is attractively valued with a forward GAAP P/E of 12.15, a 37% discount to the sector, and a forward PEG of 0.75—a 46% discount. Growth is solid, with long-term EPS growth of 16%, a 55% discount to the sector median. TIMB earns an A in profitability, with a trailing EBITDA margin of 40.3%, a 105% difference from the sector.

My next selection is a Mexican industrial group also offering plenty of growth at a reasonable price.

8. Grupo Aeroportuario del Sureste, S. A. B. de C. V. (NYSE: ASR)

  • Market Capitalization: $10.35B

  • Country: Mexico

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 15 out of 617

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 2 out of 6

Grupo Aeroportuario del Sureste (ASR) is a Mexican conglomerate operating airports in Cozumel, Huatulco, Mérida, and Oaxaca, among other regions, including South America and the Caribbean. Returning more than 30% YTD, the airport developer unlocked double-digit revenue growth last year by tapping into popular tourist destinations like Colombia and Puerto Rico and investing heavily in new infrastructure for its Cancun airport. As SA analyst Bee Spoke Investing argues:

ASR's financial resilience is evident, with 19% revenue growth in 4Q24, over $1B in cash, and a negative debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 0.3x. Despite traffic challenges in Mexico, ASR's strategic positioning and adaptability across diverse markets reinforce confidence in its future performance.

The Mexican airport developer’s valuation is surprisingly grounded considering its high growth runway, with a forward GAAP P/E of 15x, a 27% discount from the sector median. ASR’s trailing PEG of 0.51, meanwhile, comes at a 57% discount.

The company’s growth is set to take flight, with forward revenue growth of 10%, a 128% difference from the greater industrials sector. Y/Y CAPEX growth of 220.5%, a 45,053% difference from the 0.33% sector median, reflects significant updates to its existing fleet of properties while expanding its wings in new markets.

Up next is another high-flying aviation stock with a low valuation.

9. Ryanair Holdings plc (NASDAQ: RYAAY)

  • Market Capitalization: $28.7B

  • Country: Ireland

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 23 out of 617

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 25

Ryanair (RYAAY) stock is taking off. Returning 20% over the past month after an extended lull, the Irish budget airline is flying higher on a 9% passenger boost in FY25 and better-than-expected earnings results. Improved earnings and a strong FY26 outlook helped counterbalance a 16% decline in the company’s profit after tax related to higher expenses, while shares also got a boost from a buyback plan.SA analyst Dhierin Bechai explains how the carrier’s average results have helped fuel above-average returns:

Ryanair's FY25 results were underwhelming, with profit down 16% due to lower fares, but passenger growth remained strong. Aircraft delivery delays impact all airlines, limiting industry capacity growth and supporting fare stability, which benefits Ryanair's competitive position.

While its financial results may have been “underwhelming”, RYAAY’s Quant Factor Grades are more impressive. The airline boasts a forward GAAP P/E of 13.76, a 35% discount from the sector, and a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 6.9, a 38% discount. Growth is solid considering delays in aircraft orders, with a free cash flow per share growth rate of 46%, a 434% difference. Momentum is also strong, with three-month price performance of 18.8%.

Like all stocks on this list, my final emerging markets suggestion offers an attractive mix of growth at a reasonable price, geographical diversification, and long-term value.

10. América Móvil, S.A.B. de C.V. (NYSE: AMX)

  • Market Capitalization: $53.15B

  • Country: Mexico

  • Sector: Communications

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 19out of 229

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 6 out of 19

América Móvil (AMX) is a Mexican telecommunications company formed via a spin-off from Telmex (Telefonios de México), holding the largest number of mobile subscribers by a company, excluding China and India. The Mexico City-headquartered company’s forward P/E of 11.57 is 40% discounted to the sector median, one of many attractive valuation metrics.

AMX scores an A+ in profitability, with a trailing EBITDA margin of 39.5%, a 101% difference from the sector. Long-term EPS growth of 36.6% is a 254% difference from the sector, underscoring the telecom group’s potential to generate steady value over time. Like the other nine stocks discussed in this article, AMX offers global diversification while also giving you bang for your buck, and offering a great way to hedge against volatility while complementing a well-balanced portfolio.

Conclusion: For Growth at a Reasonable Price, Go Global

Trump’s trade war with China may have reached a turning point, but anxieties surrounding tariffs and inflation continue to loom over Wall Street. As a rare rally in emerging markets stocks continues into the latter half of 2025, there is value to be extracted from overlooked markets, where high-growth companies with strong fundamentals remain at low prices.

Ten emerging markets selections exhibit impressive growth outlooks at a reasonable price, and notably boast significantly higher forward growth rates and discounted valuations relative to the market.

For more stock recommendations like these, consider using Seeking Alpha's ETF Ratings Screener tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, consider joining our Alpha Picks community.

data.title: 2 BDCs To Boost Your Passive Income
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman.

We are getting through earnings season, and that has given us the latest round of business development companies (BDCs) announcing their first quarter results. As an income-focused investor, BDCs generally offer a tilt toward the higher dividend yield space. That often means they don't necessarily see their growth too much over time, but start off from a high level already. Given that, they can still be a solid fit in one's portfolio to help boost their overall passive income.

Today, we are more specifically giving 2 BDCs we own an updated look based on their latest results.

#1 Trinity Capital Inc.

Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) was one of the names that I picked up in the April market crash, which ended up being very short-lived. That ended up resulting in some quickly accumulated unrealized gains to my position. I initially entered this position in late 2021 and then added to it when it was showing some cracks in 2022. That ended up being another solid time to add.

At that time, it was some poor investments in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miners during BTC's slump. In general, 2022 was another rough year for equities, as well as more broadly, as we saw a bear market in primarily tech-related investments. That's similar to what we experienced in this latest market correction, which was relatively tech-heavy losses as their valuations had become stretched.

More interestingly, though, they recently provided their Q1 2025 results, which were mostly as good as one could expect. Total investment income grew year-over-year substantially, but that was primarily through a larger overall portfolio and ramping up their leverage. That leverage came with added interest expenses, and overall, total operating expenses also increased. That made net investment income per share come in a bit softer from the prior quarter and also the year-over-year number.

NII per share is quite important, considering that it is how the dividends are going to be covered. In this case, they continued to pay out $0.51 per quarter, so we've seen dividend coverage come under some pressure as the latest NII figure works out to 102% coverage.

They had mentioned in the latest conference call that the dividend being maintained and even growing over time is important for them.

Q. Sean-Paul Adams

Good afternoon. You guys have a pretty good track record of this stable dividend. Congrats on 21 quarters without a cut. But this quarter's NII kind of had a narrow margin versus the dividend payout. How committed are you guys when it comes to the dividend or dividend increases? And do you guys have currently have any plans to build more spillover?

A. Kyle Brown

Good question. So, a couple of things. One, the keeping for BDCs, keeping the dividends and not decreasing it, that is that's BDC 101 right there, right? So we are very focused on keeping the dividend and growing the dividend. Our board will meet and decide on whether to keep it or increase it. And our goal internally is to cover the dividend and increase that coverage over time.

They continued by mentioning that they also saw a $0.03 to $0.04 of "regular earnings per share that we just didn't see this quarter." With the current dividend yield at nearly 14.5%, it certainly is quite attractive. The fact that they are still out-earning it at that level is quite impressive, since based on the actual NAV per share, that works out closer to 15.6%.

Shares outstanding also grew considerably, but this was both a negative and a positive. In terms of the negative side for shareholders, was increased outstanding shares associated with stock awards for employees. Though that does align employees with the overall shareholder base, so it could be argued to be a positive.

Additionally, more positive for shareholders directly would have been the issuance of shares through the at-the-market offering. Since TRIN has been trading at a premium regularly, that results in this being accretive to NAV.

With that said, we had seen NAV per share decline in the latest quarter anyway. That could be another negative, but that negative impact was primarily the 6% Convertible Notes seeing an "early extinguishment."

#2 CION Investment Corporation

On the other hand, I also hold a position in CION Investment Corporation (CION), which did not report a solid earnings report with their latest announcement. This is generally a more speculative and lower quality BDC, but it is a more traditional BDC if compared to TRIN. When they recently announced, the market reacted negatively and, similar to most of the BDCs, was already under pressure from the market sell-off we saw in April.

However, when looking on a YTD basis in terms of total return comparisons between TRIN and the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), we see that CION is underperforming by a rather wide margin.

With the latest report, they posted NII of $0.36 per share, which just covers their base distribution of $0.36 exactly. However, they did maintain the same $0.36 for this quarter anyway. That has pushed the dividend yield to 15.4%. Where CION's dividend coverage is a bit more concerning is the pace at which coverage has been shrinking.

Last year, they were making supplemental distributions regularly thanks to significant distribution coverage. The latest quarter, it is now only being covered just barely, and in the prior quarter, they actually didn't cover it entirely, with NII per share at $0.35.

The distribution coverage picture looks even shakier when you consider the sizable amount of payment-in-kind. Though, they note that this is one of their unique features, and that they "strategically derive" the PIK.

As we discussed in previous quarters, the majority of our annual PIK income is strategically derived from highly structured first-lien investments or PIK income is incremental to our cash coupon. For example, we invested in litigation finance portfolios where we are our first-lien lender against the diverse mix of thousands of mass or single event cases.

We are able to attain higher yields with attractive loan-to-value structures by matching flexible PIK timing features with strict cash flow sweeps upon collections from settlements. These investments represent approximately 18% of our current PIK income.

We have started to receive increased repayments from our litigation portfolios as the long court docket delays from COVID are starting to solve and cases are being resolved, settled and distributed. Recent higher-profile cases that have settled include Gilead and Astroworld.

Approximately 64% of our PIK investments are in portfolio companies risk-rated either one or two and 99% risk-rated three or better. As a result, we believe this PIK income may not compare to restructured PIK driven by a deterioration in credit.

Two of the factors for reduced coverage of the payout have primarily been the result of the Fed cutting 100 basis points from their target rate last year, but also portfolio deterioration. In terms of the Fed cutting its target rate, CION is a bit unique in that they have more exposure to floating rate borrowings relative to other BDCs, which tend to lean toward more fixed rate borrowings.

With the latest report, nearly 74% of their outstanding leverage was based on floating interest rates. That can be important because as rates get cut, their borrowing costs will decline along with the underlying portfolio yield that they can generate from their holdings.

The NAV can reflect the portfolio declines, as the latest quarter saw a NAV per share hit of ~7.5%. There was a net unrealized depreciation of $1.21, which was the largest hit. From a year ago, the NAV has declined 11%.

On a more positive note, they did repurchase some shares during the quarter. Unlike TRIN, CION trades at a discount, and that means repurchasing shares is going to be accretive. Unfortunately, repurchasing shares means having to use cash, resulting in lower net assets, and their leverage ratio has already been increasing.

With a lower NAV, that has pushed the debt-to-equity ratio higher to 1.39x. That was up from the 1.27x last quarter and 1.03x from last year. They had also increased the total debt outstanding. At some point, some deleveraging may be necessary, and that could negatively impact NII further.

So, with a higher likelihood of needing to cut their payout, there might not be a lot of positives that one could make for wanting to own CION. I would reiterate that it is a more speculative BDC, which I keep to a smaller allocation.

That said, one of the key features that makes CION more appealing is that it is priced like a low-quality BDC with a massive 34% discount to its NAV-that was even after factoring in the latest substantial NAV hit.

That could indicate that the market continues to expect minimal or continued underperformance from CION, but if there is any sort of positive development, then shares could swing higher in a rather rapid fashion. This isn't the first time that CION has been down around these deep double-digit discounts, but when they were previously, it didn't necessarily last for that long.

Further, at this level of a discount, it appears that the market is expecting a cut in their dividend to occur. Being that it is likely priced in to some degree, it could mean fairly minimal additional declines if one is announced. Even with a cut, the yield is likely to be at least around the 10% level or higher. If one estimates a 25% reduction, we'd be looking at an 11.5% dividend yield.

Conclusion

Today, we looked at two BDCs that I hold. One of the more solid but unique venture capital-focused BDCs, TRIN, and CION, a more speculative, lower quality traditional BDC.

TRIN is internally managed and trades at a premium, which offers it the benefit of being able to issue shares accretively. It offers a solid dividend yield, though coverage has come under pressure; the management appears committed to this and optimistic going forward.

CION is an externally managed BDC, trades at a massive discount, and that is its most attractive feature. They had maintained their regular base quarterly dividend, which works out to an over 15% yield. However, I wouldn't get too comfortable with that payout as coverage has been weaker.

data.title: OppFi's Unique Business Model Could Be A Hidden Goldmine For Investors
data.description:
Here at PropNotes, we typically invest in—and write about—stocks, but consumer credit is actually one of our favorite asset classes more broadly.

It's a highly volatile industry, dependent on economic cycles, keen risk management, and the inherent incentive structures we’ve built out as a society. That said, when it comes to interest rates, you’ll be hard-pressed to find higher yields in any other asset class.

Recently, we wrote about the Discover and Capital One merger, and how we thought the newly formed company was set up to generate robust earnings growth over the next few years, largely on the back of consumer credit. Again, it’s an industry with considerable risk, but also serious potential reward.

OppFi (NYSE:OPFI), an up-and-coming consumer credit company, takes this model to the extreme, lending at the riskiest end of the spectrum to subprime consumers and the under-banked.

At first glance, some may view this approach as foolish due to the incredibly high potential for delinquency, but OPFI's prudent risk management, growing revenues, and narrowing loss ratios show that there may be a hidden goldmine in this cohort that no other public company has yet successfully tapped.

Today, we’ll take a deeper look at OPFI, explore the company's prospects, and make the case that there's considerable opportunity in this small-cap, subprime lender.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

As we just mentioned, OPFI is essentially a consumer lending company that originates loans to subprime and unbanked borrowers. This has historically been an underserved section of the market, given the inherent risk in lending to borrowers that may—or may not—ever pay you back.

That said, the company has carved out a niche and built a digital tech platform that allows those seeking loans—up to $1,750 at a time—to apply for, receive, and manage outstanding loan balances, all in one place.

Now, of course, this is a risky market, but OPFI charges rates to match, frequently quoting loan APRs in the 59% to 160% range:

Think about that.

This means that OPFI's outstanding loan assets are—on average—accumulating tremendous amounts of interest in incredibly short order.

If the company went even one year with no charge-offs, then the firm's asset base would rapidly expand, and net income would explode.

Not only that but OPFI's loan products are actually priced competitively in terms of interest rates. This adds value for stressed borrowers who want competitive rates and consolidates this income in a single, investible vehicle for investors.

Not a bad setup.

As it stands though—as of the most recent quarter—OPFI does currently write off about 35% of their loans, confirming that the company does, in fact, operate in the 'Wild West' section of the credit spectrum:

In the first quarter of 2025, OppFi's net charge-off rate improved to 35% as a percentage of revenue, compared to 48% for the prior year.

The good news is that OPFI's charge-off rates keep getting better and better. From Q1 of 2023 through to today, the company has seen a big improvement in write-off rates, which is an incredibly high point of leverage for OPFI's future earnings:

This performance led to the best quarter in the company's history, with record revenues and operating margins powering all-time highs in EPS:

More broadly, OPFI has managed to grow both top- and bottom-line results considerably over the last few years. Quarterly top-line sales have increased like clockwork as the firm's outstanding loan book has increased in size, and TTM net income—while mostly flat throughout 2023 and 2024—should see positive catalysts toward improvement in the coming quarters as the firm continues to gather data about the borrowing base.

In the most recent quarter, total revenues increased 10.1% YoY to $140 million, which is a company record. Similarly, quarterly net income increased from $10.3 million to $20.4 million YoY, representing considerable growth.

With this recent expansion, alongside management comments around business momentum, we believe that analysts—who are predicting top and bottom-line growth in the ~10% and ~30% range, respectively—could be slightly undershooting OPFI's potential:

These results build upon the improved results that OppFi has generated since 2023 and are also a result of the significant operating improvements made over that period. Notably, we expect the operating changes and investments that OppFi has made to continue generating strong results for the foreseeable future, as evidenced by our increased guidance...

We are increasing our adjusted net income guidance to $106 million to $113 million, up from the prior guidance of $95 million to $97 million, representing a 28% to 37% increase from 2024.

As we see it, as OPFI continues to improve the loss ratio through better technology, more efficient underwriting, and improved customer acquisition, the inherent interest rates with each of the outstanding loans should be allowed to cycle more quickly, allowing for a sort of 'spiral up' in terms of the firm's overall book value.

If things continue at this rate, then we could easily see $700 million or more in revenue by the end of 2026, alongside earnings per share of ~$1.60. It's hard to overstate how much growth potential there is here with these rates, loss ratios, and turnover timelines.

Valuation

As far as the valuation goes, we think OPFI is totally mispriced for this opportunity.

Currently, the stock trades at roughly 0.5x sales and roughly 9.9x forward earnings.

For a firm that has the ability to charge such spectacularly high interest rates on consumer credit, which could lead to significant top and bottom-line growth, these multiples appear incredibly cheap. Few other companies growing net income at 30% YoY are trading at sub-10 earnings multiples.

Trading at roughly $1 billion in market cap, we're hard-pressed to think of any other companies we’d rather own in this size range.

When comparing OPFI to a more fully featured, similarly sized company like Lending Club (LC), the firm's value gap stands out even more, as LC’s forward P/E comes in at more than 16x—considerably above what OPFI is currently trading for.

While this isn’t a direct comparison, even Capital One (COF), a much larger player in the market, currently trades at 13x forward earnings, and that firm is in a situation where the immediate growth opportunity is considerably smaller.

Finally, if you compare OPFI to where it has traded historically, you’ll find the current price is about in line with the five-year average price-to-book ratio, and in the middle of the range when it comes to price-to-sales, indicating that this is a completely 'reasonable' price to pay for the stock:

Overall, we would argue that OPFI's potential growth opportunity, improving loss ratios, and reasonable valuation make the stock a 'Strong Buy' in today’s market.

Risks

Of course, there are risks with any stock, and this is especially true in the case of OPFI.

Why?

Because the company is lending at the absolute lowest end of the range when it comes to consumer credit.

The reason the company can charge so much for their loans is because the cohort they're lending to—in aggregate—isn’t particularly good at paying them back. Despite this, OPFI has managed to make a good business of it, as a result of their shrewd underwriting and technology platform. However, if the economic situation worsens considerably, then we would argue that in aggregate, it would be a negative for OPFI. Sure, the company would likely see more originations from people desperate to borrow small and moderate sums, but the write-offs would likely increase, nullifying previous loan vintage profitability.

In this situation, OPFI's stock, which has already proven volatile, could come into a cyclical downturn, which would likely wipe out any recent gains to shareholders.

Not only that but OPFI—more broadly—still exists within a competitive loan environment. That’s not to say that bigger companies are currently lending in the same space, but smaller competitors and adventurous institutions may begin to chip away at OPFI's market share if the company keeps proving that there's money to be made at this level.

More competition could dent Investor sentiment, leading to multiple contraction in the share prices.

Summary

Overall, though, we see OPFI as a potential goldmine, generating incredible interest rates on an underserved cohort. Not only that but as the company disrupts more extractive, more expensive business models like payday loans, we expect market share gains and nominal growth to result in appreciation for investors.

This goes double, given the single-digit operating multiple we are currently seeing on the stock.

Taking it together, we’re happy to call OPFI a 'Strong Buy' at the current price point.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Buffett Is Doing Much More Than Selling (Part II)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Berkshire Hathaway Inc.'s (NYSE:BRK.B) The latest 13-F is out, and the company has, once again, made some meaningful sales and buys.

Most notably, the fund has sold a large stake in Bank of America (BAC), which signals that Warren Buffett and company may not be too bullish on the financial sector.

Why could that be?

In my opinion, these lines up perfectly with the view we laid out in our last Berkshire article. Namely, that the company may potentially be preparing for a few years of secular inflation/stagflation.

This is also reinforced by the fact that Berkshire keeps increasing its exposure to oil.

Overall, the fund is now very well-positioned to weather a potentially inflationary storm.

Berkshire's Latest 13-F

Berkshire/s latest 13-F reveals some meaningful moves in their portfolio.

Let's quickly break this down into Disposals, Increases and Decreases.

Stake Disposals

Citigroup Inc. (C): First acquired back in 2022 and heavily trimmed last quarter. Completely eliminated from the portfolio now.

Nu Holdings (NU): Berkshire invested $1 billion in an early funding round in 2021 and has been trimming since 2024.

Stake Increases

Occidental Petroleum (OXY): This has slowly become one of the largest positions in the portfolio as Berkshire has kept accumulating on the dip, though the increase has been small this quarter, it shows the continued commitment towards accumulating.

VeriSign Inc. (VRSN): A minor increase to the holdings.

Sirius XM Holdings (SIRI): Minor increase due to redistribution.

Constellation Brands (STZ): Slightly more than doubled their position during this quarter, which is interesting since this stock has already risen substantially.

Domino's Pizza (DPZ): 10% increase in their holdings.

Pool Corp (POOL): 145% stake increase at prices between $312/$375.

HEICO Corporation (HEI): Modest increase to their holdings.

Stake Decreases

Bank of America (BAC): This continues to be one of the company's top holdings, first established years ago through warrants. Berkshire reduced its stake by 15% last quarter and 7% this quarter.

DaVita Inc. (DVA): DVA represents 2% of Berkshire's portfolio and saw a slight trim.

Capital One Financial (COF): Purchased back in 2023, it was trimmed 18% during the last quarter and a further ~4% this quarter.

T-Mobile US (TMUS): Berkshire has sold around 15% of this position in the last 6 months.

Charter Communications (CHTR): Marginal trimming this quarter.

What Does This Mean?

A lot of things are going on, but let's focus on the big moves and what they mean.

Berkshire Ditches Banks

Buffett disposed of Citigroup and Nu, while also reducing its stake in Bank of America and Capital One.

The sale of Bank of America is especially telling.

Once the second-largest holding, it now ranks fourth. While this may have been a matter of wanting to raise more cash, we can't deny that Berkshire has shown a preference for selling financial stocks.

Why could this be?

I speculated in my last article that perhaps Berkshire was expecting a repeat of a 1970s style environment, which was characterized by instability, high rates, and inflation.

While inflation and more specifically stagflation would hurt the broader economy, financial stocks would likely be affected more.

Banks generally benefit from lower yields and a falling interest rate environment. Stagflation would likely mean higher yields and an increasing yield environment, which would erode margins and hurt growth prospects.

Here's a list of the worst-performing stocks in the 1970s.

Notice that Citigroup makes the list, returning -8.4%, and so does American Express.

Overall, I think there's a clear argument to be made that financial stocks are not a good hold during inflationary periods.

Berkshire Buys The Dip In Oil

On the contrary, oil was one of the best performers of the decade and Berkshire keeps adding to its oil exposure. The company now owns a significant amount of Occidental Petroleum, and as I highlighted in my last Berkshire post, Greg Abel has a background in the sector.

Berkshire's Top 3 Holdings Have Pricing Power

Lastly, consider that Berkshire's top 3 holdings all have pretty strong pricing power.

Apple

Apple is a household name, targeting high-end consumers who are willing to pay a premium for its products. This is a company which, in my opinion, could easily pass on the costs of inflation to its customers. That's not to say the company wouldn't be hurt during a stagflation, but it could do better than most.

American Express

By the nature of its revenues, American Express is perhaps one of the best companies to have during an inflationary environment. Its main source of revenue is credit card fees, which would automatically adjust as prices increase.

Coca-Cola

And of course, Coca-Cola (KO) is a staple that would probably do very well in a recessionary and inflationary environment. It has a unique brand and pricing power and is the kind of product that would likely hold up well during a downturn.

Final Thoughts

All in all, I believe the latest 13-F shows evidence that Berkshire is indeed positioning for a 1970s-style environment. The latest purchases and sales confirm this, and with a large cash hoard, the company is well-positioned right now to take advantage of any downturn.

data.title: RPV: Pure Value And Real Returns
data.description:
What is RPV?

The Invesco S&P 500® Pure Value ETF (NYSEARCA:RPV) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of $1.3Bn that invests in large-cap American listed stocks within a distinct value criteria. While less talked about than SPY or IVE, the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF, Invesco's offering is focused on creating a fund with pure value with legitimate criteria to provide differentiation from other basic value-based ETF's. While large fund managers like Vanguard and BlackRock have scale to provide dozens of similar offerings utilizing their brand name, RPV is a better way to get value-based equity in the U.S. The fund has been trading for almost 2 decades, and has seen strong outperformance versus other active and passively managed peers. This article seeks to compare this offering with some other larger value equity fund offerings.

RPV tracks the S&P 500 Pure Value Index, and after further review, I believe it’s one of the best - if not the best - options for investors seeking large-cap equity exposure with a true value tilt. Given today’s economic uncertainty under the current administration, RPV is worth a closer look.

Fund Breakdown

RPV was launched on March 1, 2006, by Invesco to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that replicates the S&P 500 Pure Value Index. This index, while less popular than the normal value index, has a much more comprehensive value criteria and that has proven out in past results. While the normal S&P 500 Value Index looks at price-book, price-earnings, and price-sales, the index itself is market-cap weighted. That means that larger companies can still dominate even if their value scores are not the highest (or even if it's more of a growth stock that still has a reasonable value score). Between these factors, I prefer the RPV benchmark - it's a concentrated index designed to track the performance of stocks that have the strongest value characteristics by using a score-weighting scheme. Therefore, the stocks with the highest value factors get more weighting in the fund to create a true value-based fund - and stocks with significant growth factors are excluded or have significantly reduced weighting. From an investor standpoint, the fund trades over 160,000 shares a day, and has ample liquidity for large investors. Along with its outperformance versus other "value" ETF peers, it makes this fund a great choice.

The fund also has an expense ratio of just 0.35%, only a touch higher than other value ETF's like IVE. Overall, the structure of the fund is solid and investors have been receptive to buying it to get true value equity exposure.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing RPV's portfolio, there are some significant differences in the portfolio breakdown to IVE, which is the passively managed S&P 500 value ETF. As of Mar. 31, 2025, the portfolio is completely different, with not only different weightings, but large differences in key ratios like price-book and price-earnings. RPV has almost no tech exposure, while sporting a diversified offering with health care, consumer staples and financials.

Additionally, the fund has a low P/B and P/E ratios, in line with the criteria and true purpose of the fund. This completely contrasts with IVE, the so-called value offering. IVE's average P/E is >21, while the average P/B almost doubles RPV, along with a 20% higher weighting in technology. For a fund that's supposed to be value-oriented, it's a bit confusing to see such high valuation scores in the meat of the fund.

From a risk perspective, RPV has a beta of ~1, which means that the fund will have about the same volatility as the market. While normally, I would question this, as value equity is often less volatile than high-growth stocks not trading on fundamentals, I'm not surprised given the sheer sector diversity of the fund. While the S&P 500 is concentrated in just a few large-cap tech names, RPV is an actually blended fund across several industries which have had different volatility scores post a K-shaped economic recovery post-Covid. The ETF has a Sharpe ratio of 0.13, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, though around 1 is fine as an investment. While 0.13 is a bit low, I'm not surprised given the recent up's and downs in the market. With rates probably remaining higher for longer, and tariff uncertainty, investors could move toward more stable and value focused stocks which would turn this number around quickly in the event of material outperformance versus growth.

Risks & Bottom Line

The key risk in investing in this fund is that the S&P Pure Value Index slides, which would make the investment depreciate. Recently, the market has dipped ahead of the deficit inducing tax bill passage, and I can't predict what will happen next. But the bottom line is that this fund is a true way to get value exposure - and I don't know why it doesn't get the same daily trading volume/buzz as other value equity ETF's. The fund's expense ratio is low, the fund trading volume is liquid, and investing in this ETF is worthwhile if you think that real value equity will outperform other asset classes.

data.title: A 'Large Smolt' Bakkafrost Is Transformative But Not Great Value
data.description:
P/F Bakkafrost (OTCPK:BKFKF) reported a reasonable quarter. While there was some supply pressure from better biological conditions in Norway on markets, volumes looked pretty good. Importantly, the investments Bakkafrost are making in large smolt are also progressing well, showing their possible effect already in the Scottish results. In general, the Scottish business is undergoing a total overhaul to make it more viable.

We think the earnings direction could improve markedly in the coming years even if salmon prices don't move particularly constructively for results. But even imagining considerable earnings improvements, we don't believe that the valuation case is particularly strong relative to established salmon famers in the region.

Latest Results

The dynamics of the results can be broadly explained by the fact that there has been a decline in the prices of salmon by around 15% YoY. Tax changes have again occurred, but there hasn't really been a major revision to the magnitude of taxes, which were increased by governments across the salmon producing regions in Northern Europe, but more an adjustment to stop penalizing the use of fixed contracts by salmon producers. The reason for the price declines has been an improvement in biological conditions which have been really bad in these countries for a while now, with jellyfish and sea lice harassing the fish and forcing salmon to be harvested before optimal maturity. This had a negative mix effect, and also a negative industrial effect on the pen operations. Seawater temperatures remain a little high, though, which does increase the risk of sea lie.

For the fishmeal, fish oil and fish feed segment, volumes were hit across the board as the company built up inventory. External sales from this vertically integrated operation was the first to go. It's sometimes tough for the company to source product because they rely on trawlers that they don't own of lesser fish, often pelagic fish, that get harvested as a aquaculture input and for fish oil. Prices are down here, which at least means cheaper input costs for the downstream operations.

The freshwater segment is where things get really interesting. We mentioned in our previous coverage the investments the company is making in Scotland.

The freshwater business is the operation that precedes the final stage where the premature salmon are moved to pens. It is where smolt is developed and where salmon are reproduced. The company made some investments in the Faeroes which explains the better volumes of smolt provided. But the bigger investments are the multiphase plan to make a major facility in Scotland that will change the profile of that geography considerably. In the past they've had to source some smolt from other producers because their own smolt in Scotland was inferior. They are making investments to be able to foster larger smolt that require less time to mature and therefore are exposed less to the open seas. Utilization increases, biological risks decrease, and the end salmon are larger all which contribute meaningfully to marginality.

This is the first year where their facilities are producing larger smolt, explaining the volume decline and also the margin improvement in Scotland freshwater. The volumes of smolt transferred may be down in 2025 but the average weight is supposed to double. Average size is already up 40% in Scotland for the Q1. The completion of the Applecross facility will be done by the end of 2025 hopefully. The savings so far have already been a little less than 10% of the Q1 operational EBIT. Not enough to offset the overall declines from lower prices.

While biological conditions have improved and volumes are up, the price effect has been quite severe on farming and therefore on the overall business.

But the volume increase and the better harvests this year with less premature small salmon being harvested did help the businesses even further downstream. Apparently offtake from China was strong and the industrial benefits are clear in the EBIT performance in the downstream businesses which recovered some of the farming losses.

Bottom Line

The investments in Scotland will matter a lot for the business. Run-rate adjusted FCFY for Bakkafrost is in the 6% area, which is objectively alright. EV/EBITDA is around in line with Leroy Seafood (OTCPK:LYSFF) on a two-year forward basis at around 5.5x, incorporating the additional effects from the improvements expected in the Scottish operation and in the value-added businesses downstream of Scottish farming which could be an additional 15%+ EBIT boost over the forecast figures that match industry expectations and peers who are not doing these investments. It's difficult to say exactly how larger smolt, smolt transfer rates recovered to previous levels, and all the industrial benefits therein will translate to the P/F Bakkafrost bottom line.

In all, the relative case could maybe be better, but it is a proposition with around 8-10% FCFY after investments at 2024 average prices. But there are price risks, with prices looking to be lower this year, so you're not getting quite that much objective value. The Scottish improvements are really a transformative investment and a feat of process engineering, but it's not obvious that this translates into a value case at this time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hess Corporation: Another Platform Begins Operations Soon (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Hess Corporation (NYSE:HES) announced that the fourth platform of the Guyana project is expected to begin operations later in the fiscal year. That duplicates the nearly same announcement from Exxon Mobil (XOM) the operator. This may well affect the takeover of Hess by Chevron (CVX), because the value of Hess increases as the cash flow increases. The arbitration could very easily value Hess using current values, whereas the merger agreement is already a year old. The valuation could easily begin with the larger cash flow of that fourth platform included which could make the value of Hess higher than what was agreed upon by Chevron and Hess originally.

A hearing on the arbitration that Exxon Mobil claims was triggered by the Chevron acquisition offer for Hess is now set for late May, with a decision expected a few months later. That should finally bring the current period of uncertainty to an end.

This likely overshadows the Hess earnings announcement.

Last Article

The last article noted that the advantage may lay with Exxon Mobil because the earnings of Hess grow materially with the addition of each platform. Depending upon how the hearing proceeds and the value of Hess is calculated, the cash flow usually begins at the present, which is now a year later. Lower cash flows from the past drop off, while the larger cash flows due to the addition of the fourth platform are now very close to the start. The addition of the next two platforms (about a year apart) are also closer as well.

It is very easy to see how the arbitration could come up with a higher value for Hess than what Chevron offered originally.

Also to be decided is whether the Exxon Mobil contract clause is activated to exercise this right as Exxon Mobil claims.

With this article, there is now a definite arbitration date with a likely goal as to when the decision will be made. That brings a fairly long time of uncertainty to an end.

Now, as to whether the length of time passed means that a second shareholder vote will be required is currently up for discussion. One would certainly think that, to be fair with shareholders, that should be the case. However, that course of action is far from certain. I have seen nothing that "guarantees" shareholders will get another say in this merger.

Hess Earnings

Hess earnings comparisons are currently dominated by the Guyana Project's growth rate. Therefore, the addition of the fourth platforms assures a strong of positive earnings comparisons in the near future until the next platform begins production next year. So, earnings growth is assured for some time to come.

Earnings are down from the previous fiscal year because oil prices have been steadily declining since a peak price in fiscal year 2022. The way things are going, the prices are likely to bottom within the next year or so. This project, as noted in past articles has some record low breakeven prices. So, it is likely to remain profitable when much of the industry is reporting losses.

This is a big deal for Hess, because the operator is Exxon Mobil. So, all Hess has to do is help with decision making and cash the checks as they come in.

United States Profitability

The part of the company operating in the United States has not been all that profitable. The part operated by Exxon Mobil is very profitable.

The above breakdown makes it clear that much, if not all, of the company's value is attributable to the Guyana operations operated by Exxon Mobil. This is even more remarkable considering that the North Dakota operations and the Guyana operations have the same (roughly) production levels. Together they account for roughly most of the company production.

As the Guyana project continues to grow, the need for the North Dakota operations becomes less unless there is a way found to make those operations more profitable.

Cash Flows

The joint venture cash flows will be a particularly important part of the valuation consideration. Generally offshore wells are long lived with relatively low decline rates compared with the unconventional business.

Cash flow before changes in working capital basically indicates the earnings power of operations. As was likely indicated by the net income breakdown, most of the cash flow has come from the Guyana partnership. But that also means that most of the merger value would be attributed to the Guyana Partnership. That is the cash flow that is growing the fastest over time.

Right now, the addition of platforms annually for the near future favors Exxon Mobil in the arbitration process. However, the weakening oil prices favors the Chevron position. It therefore will be interesting to see what decision the arbitration panel comes to and why.

Summary

The merger of Hess with Chevron has a fair number of uncertainties. Right now, the price of the Hess stock is tied to the actions of the Chevron stock price until this merger completes. The arbitration process could free up Hess stock to trade on its own. Long term, that could possibly be a winner for shareholders.

However, for the time being, this is a cheaper way to buy Chevron stock. While I personally prefer Exxon Mobil, the current pullback of stocks has made Chevron a buy idea as well. Therefore, this stock is a buy consideration as either a discounted way to purchase Chevron stock or as a way to trade on its own prospects. Right now, it would appear that potential shareholders win no matter what happens.

I personally do not like merger proposals that drag out this long. However, it would appear that the merger will resolve itself in the current fiscal year. Now whether it was worth the wait is up to the shareholder to decide.

Risks

With the recent stock market pullback, the risk of the merger coming undone does not appear to be a liability for shareholders as it might have been in the past. Hess has excellent prospects as an independent company. Probably the biggest risk is one or all of the parties head to court after the arbitration proceedings to drag this out longer whether or not they are allowed to based upon the arbitration agreement.

Remember, anyone can file a court action for any reason. Whether it is deemed to have substance and proceeds is another matter entirely. Court actions can be part of a negotiating process no matter what the filing looks like.

Any upstream operation is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn of those prices could materially change the outlook of the company.

This offshore project is unusually profitable. Therefore, development of Guyana will likely proceed no matter the future price outlook of oil.

data.title: Bicara: Stock Decline On FICERA Abstract Release Creates Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Bicara Therapeutics Inc. (NASDAQ:BCAX) released updated interim data from its phase 1/1b study using its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta in combination with KEYTRUDA [pembrolizumab] for the treatment of patients with 1st-line recurrent/metastatic [R/M] head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC]. Such data was released as a publication abstract on the 2025 American Society of Clinical Oncology [ASCO] Annual Meeting website. The stock price has taken a hit, and maybe it is a sell on the news event, or investors may have been expecting a more solid data release.

Regardless, this drop in stock price creates a buying opportunity. Why would that be the case? That's because I believe that this was an overblown reaction because the full data set from this phase 1/1b study has not yet been released.

This brings me to the next point, which is that the company is still on track to provide the full data set from this early-stage trial on Sunday, June 1st, 2025, at 3:00 p.m. CT/4:00 p.m. ET. This is expected to happen after it gives an oral presentation of this data at this event. The reason why I believe that this data is far more important than what has been released already is because it is going to involve an even more important efficacy endpoint compared to what has been released thus far. The company is going to release overall survival [OS] results in the full data set. Along with the release of duration of response [DOR] data. as well.

Another reason why the drop doesn't make sense is because the company has established a focus on targeting 1st-line recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients with negative HPV-disease. Not only is this an unmet medical need, but the data released far exceeds that of historical benchmarks. Plus, the company is already enrolling this very same program in the phase 2/3 FORTIFI-HN01 trial for the very same patient population. There is an alignment that interim Objective Response Rate [ORR] can be used for Accelerated Approval [AA] and then OS to be used for full approval. The downside is that full approval is going to take longer to achieve because the primary completion date of this specific study is not expected until 2029.

Interim Data Of FICERA Doesn't Tell The Entire Story

Again, it is hard to surmise the drop in stock price of Bicara Therapeutics with respect to the release of updated interim data as an abstract from its phase 1/1b study using its drug ficerafusp alfa [FICERA] to treat 1st-line recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma patients. However, I believe that this was an overblown reaction, especially since the company is still expected to release the full data set from this early-stage trial after its oral presentation at the ASCO Annual Meeting.

Before going over the interim data released from this trial, plus any catalysts still expected as part of this entire program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC] is a type of disorder characterized by the formation of malignant cells in the squamous cells of the head and neck area. This particular area can include the throat, neck, and mouth. It is important to note that it forms in the outer layer of the skin [mucous membranes], which lines the airways and intestines of a person's body. Depending upon where the cancer actually is, there can be the formation of abnormal patches and ulcers.

The global head and neck cancer market is expected to reach $8.1 billion by 2031. Another item to note is that cancer can metastasize or spread to other parts of a patient's body, such as the lungs or lymph nodes. If this happens, then the survival prognosis gets even worse and thus there needs to be new effective therapies to help these patients. Bicara believes that it may end up offering hope to them.

In order to see whether or not it is going to be able to effectively use FICERA + KEYTRUDA for the treatment of these patients with 1st-line recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC], it's running the ongoing phase 1/1b study. This particular study was intended to use this bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta antibody to treat EGFR-driven solid tumors. However, this biotech took a keen interest in specifically going after these patients with HNSCC. With that being said, the whole reason for the drop in stock price of the company was because it presented a publication of an abstract entailing updated interim results from this trial using the FICERA combination to treat these patients on the 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting website.

Honestly, the data released wasn't bad at all. Especially, in relation to the specific targeting of these 1st-line R/M HNSCC patients with HPV-negative disease. It was revealed with the negative-HPV patient population of 28 patients, with a cutoff date of December 16th of 2024, that there was a 64% Objective Response Rate [ORR] achieved. In essence, 18 out of 28 patients given this FICERA combination responded to therapy. It is my belief that data was either misinterpreted by the market itself or was a sell-on the news event. The reason why I make this claim is that the ORR noted above is an improvement above that of historical benchmarks for these HPV-negative HNSCC patients to date.

While it is not yet clear if this ORR data translates to prolonged OS, it is not going to be long for investors to see whether or not such an outcome can be obtained. This brings me to the next point, which is that Bicara Therapeutics is actually expected to release the full data set from this phase 1/1b study targeting these 1st-line R/M HNSCC HPV-negative disease on Sunday, June 1st of 2025 at 3:00 p.m. CT/4:00 p.m. ET after its oral presentation at ASCO.

Why the targeting of HPV-negative patients? That's because, in the 1st-line R/M setting, it accounts for 80% of the population. These are patients who are more than likely either smoke or chew tobacco. Plus, negative HPV for these HNSCC patients carries a worse prognosis than that of HPV-positive patients. Treatment options are not ideal and limited in that they might include: KEYTRUDA from Merck (MRK), ERBITUX [cetuximab] from Eli Lilly (LLY), and other chemotherapeutic agents. Another reason to remain bullish here is that the company is also already in the process of exploring the use of FICERA with KEYTRUDA in the ongoing phase 2/3 FORTIFI-HN01 study. This particular registration-enabling study is ideal because alignment was already achieved with respect to the FDA. In essence, it was agreed upon that an interim analysis of ORR could serve as a means to file for Accelerated Approval of FICERA for the treatment of these patients. From there, full approval could be achieved based on OS data. A good thing about this is that there might be a way for this company to obtain marketing approval for this bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta antibody a lot earlier than anticipated.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Bicara Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents of $462.1 million as of March 31st of 2025. The company is not likely to raise cash anytime soon because it has extended cash for a long time. It believes that its cash on hand will be enough for a cash runway to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2029. Of course, that is barring it doesn't need to raise cash earlier than expected to fund another corporate strategy that has yet to be revealed.

Since its inception as a company in 2018, it has raised cash through the sale of common stock with its IPO, plus other financing events such as redeemable convertible preferred stock in private placements and debt offerings. The company burns roughly $41.8 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $34.3 million in R&D expenses and then $7.5 million for G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Bicara Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the upcoming full data set from the phase 1/1b study using FICERA in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat patients with 1st-line R/M HNSCC. The reason why is that the company is expected to provide an update on OS and DOR data from this trial after its oral ASCO 2025 presentation. The risk here is that despite the company showing that this FICERA combination beat historical benchmarks in targeting these specific HNSCC patients, there is no assurance that the OS and DOR data will turn out to be positive. Nor, that the stock market itself is going to end up viewing such data in a favorable manner.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the registration phase 2/3 FORTIFI-HN01 which is also using FICERA in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat 1st-line patients with R/M HNSCC. The risk here is that even if the OS and DOR data are ideal in the June 2025 ASCO release, there is no assurance that this will translate into a similar manner upon the release of final data from this particular FORTIFI-HN01 trial. Secondly, even though the company has gained alignment with the FDA that it could possibly file for Accelerated Approval of FICERA for this specific HNSCC patient population on the basis of an interim ORR endpoint, there is no assurance that the FDA will allow this at a later time. Plus, even if Accelerated Approval of this drug is granted, the company still has to achieve a positive OS data readout to keep this drug on the market.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of a possible expansion opportunity for FICERA. This would include the fact that the biotech intends to evaluate this drug in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat HPV-positive patients with a history of smoking. Such a trial is expected to start in 2025. The risk here is that even though the FICERA combination has worked well in the targeting of HPV-negative HNSCC patients, there is no assurance that a similar positive outcome will be observed when targeting these other groups of patients.

Conclusion

Bicara Therapeutics has done well to advance its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta antibody FICERA for the treatment of patients with 1st-line R/M HNSCC. This is evidenced by the updated interim data released as part of an ASCO 2025 Abstract presentation. As I have shown above, this particular drug combination was able to achieve 64% of negative HPV patients. I believe that before punishing this company, it would be prudent to at least see if the important OS number ends up being good after the release of the full data set.

This is going to be far more important than the interim data that was just released from this phase 1/1b study for this program. With the full data set from the phase 1/1b study using FICERA in combination with KEYTRUDA targeting 1st-line R/M HNSCC patients to be released at ASCO 2025, plus the gaining of alignment with the FDA that ORR is adequate for an Accelerated Approval pathway of FICERA for these patients, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: London Stock Exchange: No Room For Error
data.description:
Following our Euronext update, we are back to comment on the London Stock Exchange Group (OTCPK:LDNXF) (OTCPK:LNSTY). Euronext has been the best-performing exchange group in the EU, although the London Stock Exchange shares have also delivered double-digit returns (including the dividend payment - Fig. 1). As a reminder, we have been buyers of LSE with a strong recommendation called This Is A Game Changer; however, given its high valuation and Forex And Interest Headwinds, we downgraded the company to neutral in September 2024.

Fig. 1

Q1 Results and Our Take

The company reported a solid Q1 2025, with both top-line sales and gross profit increasing by approximately 8% year-over-year (Fig. 2). The company experienced growth across all business lines, with the strongest performance in the Markets and Risk Intelligence segments, which grew by 13.3% and 9.6%, respectively. Revenue aligned with consensus, with momentum driven by improving Tradeweb and D&A strength. The London Stock Exchange Group also slightly improved its gross profit margin. In numbers, COGS was reported as £308 million, which was 1% higher than consensus estimates. Gross profit consequently reached £2.04 billion. Going down to the P&L, profits were up 7.8% year-on-year, ahead of the consensus of +7.6%.

Source: LSE Q1 trading update - Fig. 2

Why are we still neutral?

Still a mixed view ahead. There are pros and cons to consider.

  1. Approximately 60% of revenues and 70% of EBITDA are generated in US dollars. Therefore, the recent US dollar weakness is a negative and will likely act as an earnings headwind for the future. Having said that, the company confirmed its 2025 guidance. The company's organic constant currency sales growth outlook is between 6.5% and 7.5% in 2025. However, Q1 sales were ahead;
  2. Results were broadly aligned with expectations, so we see a limited earnings upgrade ahead. As reported in Euronext publications and taking advantage of the April markets update, we see a substantial contribution from the Markets divisions due to higher volatility recorded and the US-to-Europe equity rotation;
  3. We still believe that the company is well-positioned to win market share in the structurally growing financial data market, thanks to Workspace/Refinitiv ahead of Bloomberg. This is supported by its product offerings and improvements with new features via the Microsoft partnership. Therefore, even in a challenging macroeconomic environment, the company's earnings might be isolated from potential headcount reductions, as it typically does not charge on a per-user basis. Still, LSEG's sales base is relatively concentrated, with a significant portion generated from a few major banks and financial institutions. Following the Q1 print-out, the company confirmed no fatigue in the sales cycle and confirmed its acceleration in top-line sales growth, while improving margins. For this reason, the company projects an EBITDA increase of 50/100 basis points in 2025;
  4. The company has a buyback ongoing, and £245 million of the £500 million stock repurchase program has been completed. Additionally, in March 2025, the company repurchased $250 million of its U.S. dollar-denominated bonds with a maturity date in 2031. This is a positive move to capitalize on a longer-than-anticipated high interest rate environment;
  5. Since Brexit and following the change of the non-dom tax regime, London has experienced a decline in its appeal as a leading financial hub. This trend could become more pronounced if European stock exchanges move toward further consolidation. The city has already seen a reduction in IPO activity and a decline in the number of companies listed on its exchange.
  6. We see a drop in the company's ROIC in the last few years. This is not promising, as it might be a precursor to the company's revenue growth slowing down. LSEG boasts a class-leading FCF Yield comparable to Euronext (Fig. 3), which is particularly impressive, given the capital-intensive nature of its Refinitiv-driven data and analytics business.
